title: Phrasal Verbs in Professional and Social Life
learningObjectives: Master phrasal verbs used in professional environments
Understand phrasal verbs in social and informal contexts
Learn to distinguish between literal and figurative meanings
Practice using phrasal verbs in complex sentences

sections.title: Phrasal Verbs at the Intermediate Level
sections.content: At the B1 level, you need phrasal verbs for more complex situations — at work, in social life, and when discussing plans. Many phrasal verbs have multiple meanings depending on context. Understanding these will make your English sound much more natural and fluent.

sections.title: In the Workplace
sections.content:
Modern workplaces are full of phrasal verbs. Here's a typical day:

The team needs to come up with a new marketing strategy. The manager asks everyone to think it over before the meeting. During the meeting, they bring up several ideas and talk them through. Some ideas are turned down, but they end up choosing a creative approach. The deadline is coming up, so they need to get on with the work quickly.

Workplace phrasal verbs:
• Come up with - create/invent an idea
• Think over - consider carefully
• Bring up - mention a topic
• Talk through - discuss in detail
• Turn down - reject
• End up - finally arrive at a result
• Come up - approach (in time)
• Get on with - continue doing

sections.title: Social Situations
sections.content:
Phrasal verbs are essential in social English:

I was supposed to meet my friend, but she called off our plans because she wasn't feeling well. I decided to hang out with other friends instead. We ended up going to a new restaurant. The food was amazing — it really lived up to the reviews. We caught up on each other's news and had a great time.

Social phrasal verbs:
• Call off - cancel
• Hang out - spend time casually
• Live up to - meet expectations
• Catch up - exchange recent news
• Get along with - have a good relationship
• Fall out with - have an argument and stop being friends

sections.title: Multiple Meanings
sections.content:
Many phrasal verbs have more than one meaning:

Example 1: 'Pick up' — a) 'Pick up the phone' (answer) b) 'Pick up a language' (learn informally) c) 'Pick up someone' (collect by car)

Example 2: 'Take off' — a) 'The plane took off' (left the ground) b) 'Take off your coat' (remove) c) 'Her career took off' (became successful)

Example 3: 'Work out' — a) 'I work out at the gym' (exercise) b) 'Things worked out well' (had a good result) c) 'Work out the answer' (calculate)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Remember:
• Context determines the meaning of phrasal verbs with multiple definitions
• Professional English uses many phrasal verbs — learn them for career growth
• Social phrasal verbs make you sound natural in conversations
• Practice by reading articles and noting phrasal verbs you find
• Try to use at least one new phrasal verb each day in conversation

keyVocabulary.definition: To think of an idea or plan
keyVocabulary.example: We need to come up with a solution.
Let me think it over before I decide.

keyVocabulary.definition: To mention a topic in conversation
keyVocabulary.example: She brought up an interesting point.

keyVocabulary.definition: To reject an offer or request
keyVocabulary.example: He turned down the job offer.

keyVocabulary.definition: To cancel an event or arrangement
To exchange recent news with someone
keyVocabulary.example: Let's meet for coffee and catch up.
The movie didn't live up to the hype.
Let's get on with the project.

grammarPoints.explanation: Many common phrasal verbs have 2-3 different meanings. The context of the sentence tells you which meaning is intended.
examples: The plane took off. (= left the ground)
She took off her shoes. (= removed)
His business really took off. (= became successful)
commonMistakes: Using the wrong meaning in context: 'I need to work out the gym' — Wrong! Say 'I need to work out at the gym'
